title: Dataverse: Tunisian Construction Tech Startup Expands Internationally with BIM Platform Replic-A
summary: Tunisian startup Dataverse, founded by Wissem Jerbi, Sonia Issaad, and Mejdi Bouafif, offers a SaaS platform called Replic-A that democratizes BIM (Building Information Modeling) for construction professionals. The platform enables real-time 3D visualization, smart commenting, and AI-powered anomaly detection. Dataverse currently serves five clients across France, Benin, Algeria, Morocco, and Ivory Coast, with 2025 revenue of 300,000 TND and a team of 10. The company plans to accelerate growth in existing markets and expand further across Africa and the Middle East.
content: Fondée par trois experts du BIM et de la technologie, Wissem Jerbi (CTO), Sonia Issaad (COO) et Mejdi Bouafif (CEO), Dataverse se positionne comme une startup tunisienne innovante dans la Construction Tech. Leur ambition: rendre les technologies complexes accessibles et réellement utiles sur le terrain. Dans le secteur de la construction, les outils numériques restent souvent réservés aux experts. Résultat: communication fragmentée, erreurs détectées tardivement et coûts élevés. Dataverse apporte une réponse concrète à ces défis. Sa plateforme SaaS Replic-A permet de visualiser des maquettes 3D en temps réel, d'interagir directement dessus via des commentaires intelligents, et de détecter automatiquement les anomalies grâce à l'intelligence artificielle. L'objectif est clair: démocratiser l'usage du BIM et le rendre exploitable, même pour les profils non techniques. En centralisant les échanges autour des données, la solution améliore la coordination entre les parties prenantes, limite les erreurs et réduit les coûts liés aux retards et aux incompréhensions. Une présence déjà internationale La startup cible plusieurs marchés à fort potentiel de digitalisation, notamment la France, le Bénin, l'Algérie, le Maroc et la Côte d'Ivoire. Elle compte aujourd'hui cinq clients, principalement des cabinets spécialisés, répartis sur ces zones. En 2025, Dataverse affiche un chiffre d'affaires de 300 000 dinars et une équipe de 10 collaborateurs, avec une parité hommes-femmes, illustrant une structure agile en phase de croissance. À court terme, l'entreprise prévoit de renforcer le développement de Replic-A et d'accélérer son acquisition clients sur ses marchés actuels, notamment en France, au Bénin et en Côte d'Ivoire. À moyen terme, Dataverse vise une croissance scalable, en développant un réseau de partenaires stratégiques et en enrichissant ses solutions grâce à l'intelligence artificielle. L'expansion vers de nouveaux marchés en Afrique et au Moyen-Orient fait également partie de sa feuille de route. À plus long terme, la startup ambitionne de devenir un acteur de référence de la Construction Tech sur les marchés émergents, avec une présence progressive en Europe et à l'international.

title: ADvTECH Group Acquires Flipper International School in Ethiopia
summary: ADvTECH Group has acquired 100% of Flipper International School in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia, adding five schools and approximately 3,000 students to its portfolio. The school was founded in 1998 by Menna Selamu Bekele and Serkaddis Seifu Yeteshawork. Saham Group and Tana Africa Capital previously funded the Summit campus expansion.
content: The ADvTECH Group (ADvTECH) announced today that it has concluded an agreement to acquire a 100% interest in Flipper International School in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia, further reinforcing its position as the leading provider of private education in Africa. The acquisition will add five schools and approximately 3000 students to the group’s international portfolio. Founded in 1998 by Menna Selamu Bekele and Serkaddis Seifu Yeteshawork, Flipper International School quickly became renowned for its academic excellence. The founders' commitment to developing the Ethiopian education sector, and enhancing the country’s internationally-focused education options, led to the creation of a globally competitive, state-of-the-art school in the Summit area of Addis Ababa. The Saham Group (Saham) and Tana Africa Capital (Tana) made funding available to complete the Summit facility and bring about enhancements in technology, academics, governance and human resources. This helped boost the strong growth of Flipper International School over the last five years. Commenting on the acquisition, ADvTECH CEO Geoff Whyte said: “We are delighted to welcome Flipper International Schools to the ADvTECH Group and remain committed to its founding ethos. Furthermore, the acquisition positions us well to grow our presence in the Ethiopian market, as well as expand our scale across the continent.” Sharing their thoughts on the acquisition Menna and Serkaddis said: “We are incredibly proud of what we have built at Flipper International School. Our mission has always been to be a beacon of academic excellence in the region. We are confident that ADvTECH’s expertise and resources will help the school further enhance the educational experience and growth opportunities for students and staff.” Flipper International School’s five campuses are located in Beklobet in the centre and Summit in the East of Addis Ababa. Addis Ababa is benefitting from the rapid urbanisation in Ethiopia, leading to increased demand for high-quality education.

title: Verod Exits Tangerine APT Pensions After 4.5x AUM Growth
summary: West African private equity firm Verod has successfully exited its stake in Nigerian pension fund administrator Tangerine APT Pensions, with minority partner APT Securities and Funds Limited acquiring the shares. Under Verod's stewardship since 2020, the company's assets under management grew from ₦100 billion to over ₦445 billion (4.5x), workforce expanded from 49 to 170 employees with over 40% women, and ESG standards were strengthened aligning with IFC Performance Standards and UN SDGs.
content: Verod, a leading West African private equity firm, is pleased to announce its successful exit from pensions fund administrator, Tangerine APT Pensions Limited ("Tangerine APT Pensions"). Verod's minority partner, APT Securities and Funds Limited, is acquiring its stake. In 2020, through its funds, Verod acquired 100% of AXA Mansard Pensions Limited and 45% of APT Pension Fund Managers Limited. In 2021, the two businesses were merged to create Tangerine APT Pensions Limited. At the time of the merger, the combined Assets Under Management (AUM) stood at approximately ₦100 billion. Under Verod's stewardship, Tangerine APT Pensions experienced significant growth, with AUM increasing to over ₦445 billion. This growth reflects the company's strengthened market position driven by increases in its customer base, pension transfers and contributions from existing retirement savings account holders. Verod partnered with Tangerine APT Pensions' leadership to deliver market leading environmental, social and governance (ESG) standards with a focus on creating quality jobs, advancing gender equity and supporting local businesses. This resulted in: the company's workforce increasing from 49 people to 170 people; the workforce reaching more than 40% women with youth employment above 10%; $US4.7 million spent on local procurement, directly benefiting the domestic economy; Tangerine APT Pensions aligning with IFC Performance Standards and the UN Sustainable Development Goals. Daniel Adeoye, Partner at Verod, said: "We entered this market with a clear thesis: consolidate, optimise and scale. The numbers are compelling - AUM grew from ₦100 billion to ₦445 billion, a 4.5x increase under our stewardship. We didn't just back the company; our partnership with Tangerine APT's management team focused on operational excellence and strategic positioning in Nigeria's pension fund administration space. This exit validates Verod's investment approach. We identify high potential opportunities and work hard to execute with consistency, to deliver solid returns for our investors. This success was only possible with the support of our partners, APT Securities and Funds – we are leaving the business in very capable hands." Aliyu Dahiru, Managing Director of Tangerine APT Pensions, said: "Verod's investment transformed Tangerine APT Pensions from a single market operator into a scalable, tech-enabled pension manager with stronger governance and a clearer pathway to higher AUM and profitability. The partnership delivered stronger retirement outcomes for plan members while creating attractive returns for the shareholders."

title: Angola Sovereign Wealth Fund Commits $5M to BFA Asset Management's Kimbo Fund
summary: Angola's sovereign wealth fund FSDEA invested USD 5 million into the Kimbo Fund, Angola's first private debt fund managed by BFA Asset Management. The fund targets SMEs led by youth and women in agribusiness and logistics, aiming to raise USD 20 million total and support economic diversification.
content: On 29th of August 2024, the Angola Sovereign Wealth Fund (FSDEA) formalised its entry into the Kimbo Fund, Angola's debut private debt fund managed by BFA Asset Management (BFAAM), with an equity investment of USD 5 million. This investment will allow BFAAM's Kimbo Fund to invest in businesses with high growth potential that generate income and are tackling key challenges in the local market aligned with the national economic diversification programme, including downstream agribusiness and logistics. This equity commitment is expected to also help BFAAM to raise additional capital from other investors including the country's pension funds, high net worth individuals, insurers, and foreign institutional investors. Objectives of the Kimbo Fund: With a fundamental objective to support the growth of companies led by young people and women, Kimbo Fund aims to contribute to filling value chains, promote job creation, strengthen financial inclusion, and improve access to financing. The Fund will primarily invest in the debt components of the capital structure of small to mid-market companies, while providing investors with periodic streams of income through regular payment of distribution while prioritising the preservation of invested capital on a competitive risk-adjusted return basis. Initial investments will be around the USD 250,000-500,000 mark, followed by additional capital injections of USD1 million to support their growth. Kimbo Fund is structured as a Closed-Ended Collective Investment Scheme, regulated by the Angolan Capital Markets Commission and the Mauritius Financial Services Commission, with a tenor of Ten (10) years and a US$20 million target size. In addition to financial support, the Fund is also committed to providing training and capacity building for entrepreneurs, ensuring that their activities are aligned with global Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), whilst fostering the development of Angola's private capital investment ecosystem. Importance of the Agreement: FSDEA's investment in the Kimbo Fund represents a strong commitment to supporting the growth of SMEs in Angola, which are crucial to the country's economic development. This agreement not only symbolises the mutual trust between the two institutions but also highlights the vital role both play in creating a more dynamic and resilient financial environment in Angola. The collaboration between FSDEA and BFAAM aligns with the long-term vision of both institutions to foster a more inclusive, sustainable, and diversified financial markets in the country. Armando Manuel, Chairman of the Board of Directors of FSDEA, highlighted the importance of this agreement, stating: "FSDEA's investment in this Venture Capital Fund is a strategic step that reinforces our commitment to supporting the growth of small and medium-sized enterprises in Angola through third-party entities. This initiative not only contributes to strengthening the national economy but also promotes financial inclusion, encouraging entrepreneurship and innovation in various areas, especially among women and young people." Rui Oliveira, Chief Executive Officer & Co-CIO of BFAAM, also commented on the relevance of this partnership: "We are excited about this collaboration with FSDEA, which will have a significant positive impact on Angola's economic and social development. The Kimbo Fund is a testament to our investment thesis and long-term mission to use innovative capital solutions as a vehicle to finance and build stronger companies and produce measurable impact, as well as further cement our commitment to the growth of the region's capital markets." The ceremony was attended by the leadership of both institutions. From FSDEA, attendees included Armando Manuel, Chairman of the Board of Directors; Alcides Safeca, Executive Director; Igor Lima, Executive Director; Rui Ceita, Executive Director; Gabriel Silva, Executive Director; Alcídio José, Head of Strategy and Business Office; and David Zilungo, Head of the Legal Department. Representing BFAAM, attendees included Rui Oliveira, CEO & Co-CIO; Carla de Jesus, Executive Director; Manuel André, Executive Director; Pascoal Martinho, Investment Associate; and Cássia André, Executive Assistant & Office Manager. The event was witnessed by Ms. Nádia Pinto, Acting Chairperson of the Capital Markets Commission (CMC). About FSDEA: The Angolan Sovereign Wealth Fund (FSDEA) is an instrument for saving and transferring wealth to future generations, as well as maximizing returns, contributing to the economic growth of the country and the continent. FSDEA plays a complementary role in expanding efforts for economic diversification, in line with the directives of the National Development Plan (PND) approved by the Government. About BFAAM: BFA Asset Management is an investment management company founded in Angola in 2016, with a cumulative of USD 500 million in assets under management.

title: Oasis Capital exits investment in MANSA BANK Côte d'Ivoire
summary: West African SME growth investor Oasis Capital has exited its investment in MANSA BANK Côte d'Ivoire, marking its first full exit in the Francophone market and second exit from Oasis Africa Fund I. Oasis invested in 2021 to strengthen the bank's capital base. MANSA BANK, founded in 2020, has become one of Côte d'Ivoire's fastest-growing banks using a high-quality single-branch model powered by technology.
content: Abidjan, Côte d’Ivoire – Oasis Capital, an SME growth investor in West Africa, is proud to announce the exit of its investment in MANSA BANK Côte d’Ivoire, a fast-growing bank in Côte d’Ivoire. This exit marks the first full exit of Oasis in the Francophone market and the second of the Oasis Africa Fund I portfolio. Oasis invested in MANSA BANK in 2021 to strengthen its capital base for operations in Côte d’Ivoire. Named after the great Mali Empire of Mansa Musa, MANSA BANK opened its doors in 2020 and has grown rapidly within its 5-year life to become one of the fastest-growing banks in the country. MANSA BANK also sponsored “JEUNES MANSA”, a seminal TV program aimed at promoting entrepreneurship, which has supported more than 100 young African entrepreneurs. MANSA BANK’s impressive growth has been powered by its strategy of operating a high-quality single-branch model while leveraging the power of technology to pursue growth. Mr. Matthew Boadu Adjei, Founder and CEO of Oasis, remarks, "We are proud to have partnered with El-Hassana and his team during the early years of MANSA’s life. Africa needs more entrepreneurs like the MANSA team, and our capital is ready to support many more entrepreneurs to the benefit of Africa." The Founder and CEO of MANSA BANK, El-Hassana KABA, stated that “the exit of OASIS from the Bank’s shareholding structure was mutually agreed upon by both parties. We are grateful for the valuable partnership with OASIS and for the positive contribution made during its time as a shareholder.” "OASIS’s participation in MANSA BANK’s capital significantly strengthened the Bank’s financial structure and supported the growth of its activities. We extend our sincere thanks to Matthew and his team for his unwavering support and commitment throughout the partnership."

title: Development Partners International invests $190 million in Egypt's Alameda Healthcare
summary: Development Partners International (DPI) and co-investors have signed a binding agreement for a $190 million minority stake investment in Alameda Healthcare, Egypt's leading private healthcare group. The investment, the largest healthcare deal of its kind in Egypt, will fund Alameda's expansion across Egypt and the GCC region, including new hospitals, advanced technologies, and Centers of Excellence. Alameda's Chairman Dr. Fahad Khater remains majority shareholder.
content: Development Partners International (DPI), through funds it advises, and co-investors have signed a binding agreement for a $190 million minority stake investment in Alameda Healthcare, Egypt's leading private healthcare group. The transaction, primarily a capital increase, is subject to regulatory approvals and expected to close in Q3 2025. This marks the largest healthcare investment of its kind in Egypt to date. The funding will accelerate Alameda's expansion within Egypt and across the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) region, including Saudi Arabia and the UAE. Proceeds will be used to expand Alameda's footprint and capacity, invest in advanced technologies, and scale its Centers of Excellence. DPI will collaborate closely with Alameda's executive team led by Chairman Dr. Fahad Khater, who retains majority ownership. Established in 1999, Alameda operates over 1,000 beds across hospitals under the As-Salam International and Dar Al Fouad brands, 7 polyclinics, specialized centers (Elixir Gastro and Liver Care Center, German Rehabilitation Center), home healthcare service Tabibi 24/7, and in-house diagnostics, labs, and pharmacies. All Alameda hospitals are JCI-accredited, with four of Egypt's five private JCI-accredited hospitals belonging to Alameda. The investment aims to increase access to high-quality healthcare, enhance training for medical professionals, and support Egypt's emergence as a regional hub for medical value travel.

title: Tunisia Unveils Comprehensive Startup Support Framework
summary: Tunisia has launched a unique legal framework for startups, a dedicated program to support startup enablers and entrepreneurs, and a pioneering initiative to boost venture capital activity in the country.
content: A unique legal framework for startups. Helping those who help startups & entrepreneurs. A one-of-a-kind program to boost venture capital. Get more details and get in touch with us. Zoom in on the startup galaxy signals.

title: AfricaCrypt 2026 : Conférence internationale sur la cryptographie et la cybersécurité à Hammamet
summary: La 17e édition d'AfricaCrypt 2026 se tiendra à Hammamet, en Tunisie, du 8 au 10 juillet 2026. Organisée par l'International Association for Cryptologic Research (IACR) en partenariat avec l'Université de Tunis et le Centre tunisien de recherche militaire, cette conférence réunira des experts internationaux pour discuter des avancées en cryptographie, cybersécurité et cryptographie post-quantique. Une école d'été sur la cryptographie post-quantique précédera l'événement les 6 et 7 juillet.
content: La 17e édition d'AfricaCrypt 2026, la conférence internationale annuelle dédiée à la cryptographie et à la cybersécurité, se déroulera à Hammamet du 8 au 10 juillet. Organisé à l'initiative de l'International Association for Cryptologic Research (IACR), cet événement réunira des chercheurs, experts et universitaires venus de plusieurs pays pour débattre des enjeux actuels de la sécurité numérique. Cette nouvelle édition mettra en lumière les avancées scientifiques et technologiques les plus récentes dans les domaines de la cryptographie et de la cybersécurité. Les participants échangeront sur les défis posés par l'évolution rapide des technologies numériques, notamment l'émergence de l'informatique quantique, qui remet progressivement en question les systèmes cryptographiques traditionnels. Les discussions porteront, en outre, sur le développement de solutions innovantes destinées à protéger les infrastructures critiques et les données sensibles et sur la transition vers les nouvelles normes de cryptographie post-quantique. La conférence est organisée conjointement par l'Université de Tunis et le Centre tunisien de recherche militaire, avec le soutien de la Société tunisienne de mathématiques et de la Société méditerranéenne des sciences mathématiques, en collaboration avec les écoles doctorales des universités de Monastir et de Sousse. Elle réunira plusieurs spécialistes de renommée internationale, confirmant ainsi le rôle d'AfricaCrypt comme l'un des principaux rendez-vous scientifiques consacrés à la sécurité de l'information sur le continent africain. En prélude à la conférence, une école d'été dédiée à la cryptographie post-quantique se tiendra les 6 et 7 juillet. Destinée aux jeunes chercheurs d'Afrique et d'ailleurs, cette formation abordera les fondements de la cryptographie de nouvelle génération et présentera les principales familles d'algorithmes conçues pour résister aux futures capacités de calcul des ordinateurs quantiques. L'objectif est de préparer la relève scientifique aux défis de la cybersécurité de demain et d'encourager le développement de recherches innovantes dans ce domaine stratégique.

title: CEPEX launches call for Tunisian companies to participate in ELECTRONICA 2026 in Munich
summary: The Centre de Promotion des Exportations (CEPEX) is organizing a Tunisian pavilion at ELECTRONICA 2026, the world's leading electronics trade fair, taking place from November 10-13, 2026 in Munich, Germany. The call targets Tunisian companies in electronics, embedded systems, industrial automation, IoT, Industry 4.0, and advanced technologies.
content: Le Centre de Promotion des Exportations (CEPEX) organisera la participation des entreprises tunisiennes au Salon « ELECTRONICA 2026 » qui se tiendra, du 10 au 13 novembre 2026, à Munich en Allemagne. Le CEPEX a lancé dans ce cadre un appel à participation à l'adresse des entreprises tunisiennes opérant dans les secteurs de l'électronique, des cartes électroniques, des systèmes embarqués, de l'automatisation industrielle, de l'IoT et objets connectés, des solutions intelligentes et Industrie 4.0 et des technologies de pointe. ELECTRONICA se veut un rendez-vous mondial incontournable de l'industrie électronique, a rappelé le CEPEX. Il réunit les principaux donneurs d'ordre, fabricants, intégrateurs, investisseurs et décideurs internationaux à la recherche de nouvelles technologies, de partenaires industriels et de fournisseurs innovants. La participation au salon offrira l'opportunité aux entreprises tunisiennes de rencontrer les leaders mondiaux de l'électronique et des technologies avancées, de développer de nouvelles opportunités d'affaires à l'international et de valoriser le savoir-faire tunisien auprès d'un public professionnel qualifié. Elle favorisera également l'accès aux dernières tendances technologiques et innovations du secteur et le renforcement de la présence des entreprises tunisiennes sur les marchés européens et mondiaux.

title: 20 startups tunisiennes participent à l'édition virtuelle du salon Web Summit à Lisbonne
summary: Le Centre de Promotion des Exportations (CEPEX) en partenariat avec Ernst & Young organise la participation officielle de 20 startups tunisiennes à l'édition virtuelle du Web Summit, du 2 au 4 décembre 2020 à Lisbonne. Plus de 2500 startups, 1500 investisseurs et 800 conférenciers sont attendus. Les startups sélectionnées bénéficieront de visibilité, d'accès à la plateforme de networking, de réunions avec investisseurs et de workshops.
content: Le Centre de Promotion des Exportations organise en partenariat avec le bureau de consulting Ernst & Young, la participation officielle de 20 startups tunisiennes à l'édition virtuelle du salon Web Summit, prévue du 02 au 04 Décembre 2020 à Lisbonne. Considéré comme la plus grande conférence technologique en Europe, le Web Summit est un mélange atypique de salon professionnel, de conférences et d'évènements de networking. Plus de 2500 startups, 1500 investisseurs et 800 conférenciers seront attendus lors de cette édition virtuelle. Les startups tunisiennes innovantes avec un fort potentiel à l'international peuvent déposer leurs candidatures. Les 20 startups sélectionnées auront l'occasion d'accroître leur visibilité, de rencontrer des partenaires et de faire du réseautage avec des contacts qualifiés, en bénéficiant d'une visibilité sur la page web de l'événement, d'un accès à l'application de réseautage de l'événement et de la participation aux réunions d'investisseurs (investors meeting matchmaking platform) ainsi qu'aux différents workshops.

title: Winners of Nedbank N*ovation Data and Analytics Masters Challenge Announced
summary: Nedbank announced winners of its 2026 N*ovation Data and Analytics Masters Challenge in South Africa, awarding ZAR500,000 in prizes and offering fast-tracked recruitment to top data talent.
content: South Africa’s Nedbank has announced the winners of the Nedbank N*ovation Data and Analytics Masters Challenge 2026, following weeks of rigorous virtual competition and an in-person finale in Johannesburg. The national Data and Analytics Challenge is designed to identify and recruit South Africa’s most advanced data and analytics practitioners, and this year’s edition culminated in a live contest where finalists were tested on their ability to solve complex, real-world banking problems. The winner of the data engineering track was Mogale Thobejane, with Tshifhiwa Gift Moila and Tinashe Chinyati coming in second and third place. The data science and machine learning track was won by Edward Rycroft, with Alex Jacobs in second and Vusani Khosa in third. Contestants shared ZAR500,000 (US$30,000) in prize money. The top performers will benefit from a fast-tracked recruitment process, highlighting the bank’s ongoing investment in building future-ready data and analytics capabilities. In collaboration with partners including OfferZen, Zindi, Otinga, and Microsoft, the challenge potentially connects top talent directly with career opportunities. “This challenge is about more than competition, it reflects our belief that data excellence is a strategic capability, and this challenge was a novel one with a positive outcome demonstrated by the level of quality on display at the challenge,” said Nedbank Group technology executive Thando Lukhele.

title: CRTEn and ANME Extend Call for Applications for Innovation in Ecological Transition to June 19, 2025
summary: The Centre de recherches et des technologies de l'énergie (CRTEn) and the Agence nationale pour la maîtrise de l'énergie (ANME) have extended the deadline for their call for applications rewarding innovative projects in energy transition and ecology to June 19, 2025. The call targets researchers, students, startup founders, and project holders in renewable energy, decarbonization, energy efficiency, green hydrogen, green innovation, and clean tech. Winners will receive trophies, certified carbon accounting training, incubation support, and other benefits.
content: The deadline for the call for applications launched by the Centre de recherches et des technologies de l'énergie (CRTEn) and the Agence nationale pour la maîtrise de l'énergie (ANME) to reward the most innovative projects in fields related to energy and ecological transition has been postponed to June 19, 2025, according to the Société de Gestion de la Technopole de Borj Cédria. This call addresses researchers, students, startup founders, or holders of innovative projects in areas linked to renewable energies, decarbonization, energy and ecological efficiency, green hydrogen, green innovation, and clean technologies. It aims to promote technological, scientific, and entrepreneurial innovation carried by the constituent structures of the Technopole de Borj Cédria, highlight innovative projects in sustainable energy and clean technologies, and reward initiatives with strong potential for ecological and technological transformation and impact on local communities, employment, or energy access. Candidates must be affiliated with a structure located within the technopole (research centers, higher education institutions). Projects must present a level of technological maturity (at least validated at laboratory scale) relevant for valorization or transfer to the economic fabric. The award ceremony will take place on June 25, 2025, at the Technopole de Borj Cédria. Prizes will be awarded according to different project categories: innovative technological or entrepreneurial solutions for startup founders, applied research work for researchers, and supported academic projects (PFE, master, thesis) for students. One winner per category will receive an excellence trophy, a certified Carbon Accounting training funded by ANME, an official certificate of recognition. They will also benefit from free hosting and support in the business incubator for one year for researchers and students wishing to become startup founders, as well as training in life cycle analysis, photovoltaic systems, project mounting, and financing.

title: Tunisia Ranks 2nd in North Africa in Global Startup Ecosystem Index 2023
summary: Tunisia ranked 2nd in North Africa (91st globally) in the 2023 Global Startup Ecosystem Index by StartupBlink, while Tunis improved 57 places to 360th globally, becoming the 20th ranked ecosystem in Africa and the Middle East. The rise is attributed to Morocco's steeper decline. Tunisia's startup ecosystem remains in early development, but tech startup funding grew significantly in 2022. Government initiatives like Startup Tunisia, the 2018 Startup Act, and the Startup Label provide tax exemptions and legal support. International backing includes World Bank projects such as InnovaTech and the Anava Fund of Funds, which aims to invest in over 13 seed and early-stage VC funds.
content: La Tunisie a retrouvé sa place de 2ème pays en Afrique du Nord, malgré une baisse de 8 places dans l’indice, pour se classer au 91 ème rang mondial. Cette remontée dans le classement de l’Afrique du Nord est principalement, selon les analystes du GSE Index (Global Startup Ecosystem) publié sur le site « StartupBlink » due au déclin du Maroc, qui a été plus marqué que celui de la Tunisie. La Tunisie a baissé dans l’indice chaque année, depuis l’introduction de l’indice en 2017. Tunis, seule ville tunisienne classée, a affiché une amélioration notable de 57 places pour se classer au 360ème rang mondial. Tunis avance d’une place en Afrique du Nord pour retrouver la 2ème position, dépassant Casablanca qui avait pris cette position de Tunis en 2022. Tunis est désormais le 20 ème écosystème le mieux classé en Afrique et Moyen-Orient, après avoir gagné une place au niveau régional et 10 ème en Afrique après avoir perdu une place. La dynamique inverse de Tunis et de la Tunisie s’explique par le fait que ce classement des pays prend en compte la taille de la population tandis que la ville le classement ne le fait pas. Le classement du pays est également influencé par le fait qu’il n’y a pas d’autres écosystèmes semenciers classés dans le pays en dehors de Tunis. L’écosystème tunisien des startups en est encore à la première phase de son développement, mais le financement total des startups technologiques tunisiennes a considérablement augmenté en 2022. Le gouvernement tunisien est bien conscient de l’importance de créer des hubs de startups, et a mis en place l’organisation du soutien aux startups, « Startup Tunisie ». L’organisation a adopté une loi sur les startups en 2018, qui est un cadre juridique conçu pour stimuler l’innovation et favoriser l’entrepreneuriat. Une autre initiative de Startup Tunisie, le « Startup Label », est un statut spécial qui permet aux startups d’être exonérées de l’impôt sur les plus-values jusqu’à huit ans. De nombreuses organisations internationales soutiennent l’écosystème des startups en Tunisie. Par exemple, la Banque mondiale apporte un soutien aux femmes et aux PME en Tunisie à travers le Projet Startups Innovantes et PME, et soutient activement Startup Tunisie. Elle a lancé deux fonds PME à travers le Projet Tunisien Startups et PME Innovantes. Le premier est InnovaTech, qui se concentre sur les investissements dans l’innovation et la technologie des médias numériques, les énergies renouvelables, l’agro-industrie, les TIC, les médias et le commerce électronique, ainsi que la transformation numérique. Le second est un fonds commun de la Banque mondiale, de Smart Capital, du CDC et de la GIZ appelé Anava Fund of Funds qui vise à investir plus de 13 fonds de capital-risque dédiés aux startups en amorçage et en démarrage. Grâce à sa proximité avec le Moyen-Orient, le reste de l’Afrique et l’Europe, la Tunisie a la capacité d’établir des relations commerciales internationales de grande envergure. Pour se développer, l’écosystème des startups tunisiennes doit atteindre une masse critique de startups de haute qualité.

title: Cascador invests over $5M in 7 Nigerian tech startups
summary: Africa-focused platform Cascador deployed over US$5 million across seven growth-stage startups at its second annual Pitch Day, bringing total Pitch Day funding to over US$9 million in two years. The Catalytic Fund provided debt and equity to ventures including Agriarche, Koolboks, Powerstove, First Electric, Fortics, Stears, and Indigenius AI.
content: Cascador, an Africa-focused platform for growth-stage founders building businesses with social impact, has deployed over US$5 million into seven innovative startups at its second annual pitch day. Since its founding in 2019, Cascador has supported over 60 entrepreneurs who have collectively raised more than US$55 million in capital. The company hosted its second annual Pitch Day on June 3, where over US$5 million of growth capital was deployed to seven innovative African entrepreneurs. The funding recipients included Agriarche, which secured US$1.7 million in debt funding; Koolboks, which banked US$1.4 million in debt; Powerstove, which secured debt funding worth US$1.2 million; and First Electric, which raised US$357,000 in debt. The other funded ventures were Fortics, which raised US$142,000 in debt; Stears, which banked US$450,000 in equity funding; and Indigenius AI, which raised US$250,000 in equity investment. “In just two years, Pitch Day has awarded more than US$9 million to growth-stage African founders, helping to build a new generation of entrepreneurs equipped to scale transformative businesses. We’re now looking for the next cohort of exceptional founders to join our 2026 ScaleUp programme and hope to see them on stage at the next Pitch Day,” said Dave DeLucia, founder of Cascador. “Cascador’s ScaleUp programme built upon my team’s ability to translate learning into action by helping us refine our message and market position, adjust our funding strategy, and adapt without defensiveness. The Catalytic Fund due diligence team assessed Agriarche’s financial strength, resourcefulness, and track record of success, and they rewarded our high-potential for scale and impact today by awarding a new NGN2.5 billion credit facility to power our growth,” said Deina Mayaki of Agriarche, the largest funding recipient from the 2026 Catalytic Fund.

title: Holocene closes Southern Africa's first dedicated climate tech fund
summary: VC firm Holocene announced the final close of Holocene Ventures Fund I (HVF1), Southern Africa's first dedicated high-growth climate tech fund. The fund has backed 10 companies in 18 months, creating over 500 jobs, achieving a 2x markup on invested capital, and attracting $8 in follow-on capital for every $1 invested.
content: VC firm Holocene has announced the final close of Holocene Ventures Fund I (HVF1), Southern Africa’s first dedicated high-growth climate tech fund. Holocene targets promising, local founders at the earliest stages, but who lack capital and hands-on operational support to get them to scale, especially in a nascent sector such as climate-tech. Through HVF1, it has backed 10 companies in the 18 months it has been deployed, creating over 500 jobs and achieving a 2x markup on invested capital, while attracting US$8 in follow-on capital for every US$1 it invested. “It’s clear we need to dramatically accelerate the pace of climate innovation in Africa,” founder Josh Romisher said. “The continent will double in size, urbanise, and begin to truly consume during our lifetimes. That can be seen as an impending climate catastrophe or a massive innovation opportunity. We choose to view it as the latter.” Portfolio ventures include FARO, a circular economy company built around technology-enabled supply chains for unused inventory; ScootHero, which is deploying over 500 electric motorbikes and more than 50 battery-swap stations across South Africa; and Yongeza, an e-mobility infrastructure company in Uganda. Each investment comes with at least 12 months of embedded, high touch support from a team of globally trained specialists: operators, executives-in-residence, and experts in revenue growth, fundraising, and grant writing. “What excites me most is not just that Holocene backed us early, it’s that they got in the trenches with us,” said Wahlied Cole, founder and CEO of ScootHero. “When we needed help with go-to-market, they brought in operators. When we were hitting a ceiling on capital, they helped us unlock grants and asset finance. It wasn’t just capital. It was a partnership built on real understanding of what African founders need.” Jonathan Smit, exited founder and HVF1 investor, said he invested in Holocene because he believes climate innovation will be one of the defining challenges and opportunities of our generation, particularly in Africa. “What stood out to me was not just the investment thesis, but the quality of the team and the hands-on way they support founders. Holocene is helping build the ecosystem and companies that can deliver meaningful climate impact while creating lasting economic value,” he said. The final close marks the beginning of the fund’s value-creation phase — the work of driving portfolio companies towards scale and exits. “We’ve shown that the intersection of climate impact and financial returns is real,” Romisher said. “The task now is to scale that proof. We live the Stanford motto: change lives, change organisations, and change the world. That’s not always easy, but it is deeply meaningful.”

title: African agrifood startups can apply for $1M THRIVE Global Impact Challenge
summary: African agrifood startups are invited to apply for the THRIVE Global Impact Challenge, a global competition supporting sustainable agriculture aligned with UN SDGs. The winner receives up to $1 million investment from SVG Ventures and a chance to pitch at the THRIVE Global Impact Summit in Silicon Valley. Applications close May 25.
content: African agrifood startups are invited to apply for the THRIVE Global Impact Challenge, which is aimed at supporting the transition to a net-zero future for agriculture. The THRIVE Global Impact Challenge is a global search for innovative startups advancing a more sustainable future for agriculture. Aligned with the UN’s Sustainable Development Goals, the Global Impact Challenge is focused on five key themes. Finalists from each theme will advance to the finals and pitch at the THRIVE Global Impact Summit, where the overall winner will be announced. The winning company receives investment of up to US$1 Million from SVG Ventures, and the opportunity to pitch live at THRIVE Global Impact Summit in Silicon Valley. Applications are open here until May 25. Passionate about the vibrant tech startups scene in Africa, Tom can usually be found sniffing out the continent's most exciting new companies and entrepreneurs, funding rounds and any other developments within the growing ecosystem. Comments are closed.

title: ClawCon Nairobi: Free Community-Led AI Event for African Startups
summary: African startups are invited to ClawCon Nairobi, a free community-led gathering on June 18 at Strathmore University focused on personal AI, agents, workflows, and OpenClaw tools. The event features live demos, founder presentations, and discussions on AI sovereignty for Nairobi's tech ecosystem.
content: African startups are invited to attend ClawCon Nairobi, a free, community-led gathering focused on personal AI, agents, workflows, OpenClaw, and the emerging tools that let individuals build more powerful digital systems for themselves. Set for June 18 at Strathmore University, ClawCon is designed for builders, founders, developers, creators, operators, researchers, students, and AI-curious people. It will feature live demos, founder presentations, Q&As, and conversations around what personal AI could mean for Nairobi's tech ecosystem. The event was born from the OpenClaw community, and its mission to put power back into people's hands – giving individuals more sovereignty over personal AI, their workflows, their tools, and their digital lives. Register to attend here.

title: Tunisia's RoboCare Secures Funding to Accelerate Precision Agriculture in Africa and Middle East
summary: Tunisian precision agriculture startup RoboCare raises a six-figure investment from 216 Capital to expand its AI-driven agricultural management platform across African and Middle Eastern markets.
content: Tunisian startup RoboCare, which specialises in precision agriculture and artificial intelligence applied to the agricultural sector, has secured a six-figure investment from 216 Capital. Founded in Sfax, RoboCare develops an agricultural management platform that helps farmers make better decisions through the intelligent use of multiple data sources – satellite imagery, drone data, IoT sensors, weather data, and field expertise. Through its AI models, the solution enables early detection of crop diseases and stress, optimises resource usage, and improves farm performance. RoboCare has now raised a six-figure funding round from 216 Capital to support its next growth phase and expansion into African and Middle Eastern markets. “For 216 Capital, this investment fully aligns with its strategy of supporting high-potential tech startups capable of delivering concrete answers to the continent’s major economic, social, and environmental challenges,” said Hassen Arfaoui, principal at 216 Capital.

title: South Africa's Kloset Klub raises seed funding from Thinkroom and Frank Smit
summary: South African circular fashion platform Kloset Klub has secured an undisclosed seed funding round led by African entrepreneurship firm Thinkroom and co-investor Frank Smit to expand its curated resale and peer-to-peer marketplace for pre-loved clothing.
content: South Africa's Kloset Klub, a fast-growing circular fashion platform, has secured a round of seed funding to help it expand operations. Founded by Phumi Körber, Kloset Klub helps people buy and sell quality pre-loved clothing through curated resale, peer-to-peer shopping, and managed closet services. The platform helps sellers unlock value from their wardrobes while giving buyers access to premium pre-loved fashion at more accessible prices. The startup has now raised an undisclosed amount of seed funding from Thinkroom, an African entrepreneurship and innovation firm, and co-investor Frank Smit. "This investment is more than just capital, it's a vote of confidence in the journey we've been on," said Körber. "Building a business is never linear, and having partners like Thinkroom and Frank, who understand that reality, makes a huge difference. We're excited to keep building something that not only looks good, but does good too." "We've watched Phumi build Kloset Klub through real challenges, real pivots, requiring real grit," said Catherine Young, founder of Thinkroom. "Phumi's resilience and her approach to building a business stood out early on. We are incredibly proud to be writing the first cheque into Kloset Klub and to support Phumi as she scales."

title: South African Startup Palé Selected for Grindstone Ventures and MIC Female Founder Funding Programme
summary: South African AI-powered beverage technology platform Palé, founded by Mashokane Ramusetheli, has been selected as the third startup for the female founder funding-readiness initiative run by Grindstone Ventures and the Mineworkers Investment Company (MIC). The programme aims to fast-track growth and funding-readiness of black female founders with MIC providing capital and Grindstone offering venture-building support.
content: South African startup Palé, an innovative black female-owned beverage technology platform, is the third startup selected for the pioneering female founder funding-readiness initiative run by Grindstone Ventures and the Mineworkers Investment Company (MIC). Grindstone is a global accelerator founded in 2013, engineering high-growth entrepreneurship and co-owned by Knife Capital and Thinkroom. The Mineworkers Investment Company (MIC), meanwhile, is a 100 per cent black-owned investment company established in 1995 by the Mineworkers Investment Trust (MIT) to create a sustainable asset base for the benefit of mine, energy and construction workers and their dependents. The female-focused programme run by the two was launched to fast-track the growth and funding-readiness of exceptional black female founders, with MIC committing capital and Grindstone providing tailored venture-building support. Palé is the third startup selected for the programme. Founded by Mashokane Ramusetheli, Palé is a proudly South African, AI-powered “pocket sommelier” that delivers hyper-personalised wine and cocktail recommendations to users based on their unique flavour profiles. Through strategic partnerships with global beverage leaders such as Pernod Ricard and a growing presence at high profile festivals, Palé is reshaping the way consumers engage with beverages, while unlocking data and marketing value for restaurants, retailers, and alcohol brands. “We’re excited to welcome Mash and the Palé team into the programme,” said Thandiwe Maqetuka, CEO of Grindstone Ventures. “Mash has built a truly impressive platform, one that not only uses technology to elevate consumer experience, but also champions responsible drinking, promotes proudly South African wine brands, and actively shifts the beverage industry towards greater inclusivity. That’s the kind of vision and grit we back at Grindstone.” Selected through Grindstone’s rigorous and multi-stage due diligence process, Palé stood out for its clear value proposition, scalable tech platform, and strong founder-market fit. With early traction, strategic B2B partnerships, and a differentiated product in a rapidly evolving industry, the business is well-positioned for commercial expansion and future investment. “This partnership with Grindstone Ventures and MIC is incredibly meaningful,” said Ramusetheli. “It’s more than just funding, it’s a catalyst for impact. Palé is about making the wine and cocktail space more inclusive, more intelligent, and more culturally relevant. Being recognised and supported in this way not only validates our mission but helps us scale it further.”

title: Nigerian AI Travel Platform Viewlocked Launches to Unify Trip Planning
summary: Nigerian startup Viewlocked, founded in 2025 by Abbey Ola, has launched an AI-powered travel planning platform that aggregates accommodations, experiences, transportation, and local services into a single interface. The bootstrapped company has onboarded travel partners across 15 countries and is preparing for a seed funding round to fuel product development and global expansion.
content: Nigerian startup Viewlocked is an AI-powered travel planning platform that helps travellers discover, organise, and eventually book every part of a trip in one place. Founded in 2025 by Abbey Ola, Viewlocked provides travel businesses with a platform to showcase their services to travellers who are actively planning trips, and helps travellers plan and pay for their trips. “Today, planning a trip often requires jumping between multiple websites and apps to find accommodations, experiences, transportation, guides, rentals, and local services. Viewlocked brings these elements together into a single platform, allowing travellers to plan more efficiently while discovering trusted local businesses and unique experiences,” Ola said. The idea emerged, Ola said, from observing how fragmented travel planning remains despite advances in travel technology. “Travellers often spend significant time switching between platforms to build a single itinerary, while many local travel businesses struggle to gain visibility outside of major booking platforms,” he said. “The gap we identified was fragmentation. Most travel platforms solve only one piece of the journey. Travellers may use one platform for accommodations, another for experiences, another for transportation, and additional tools for itinerary planning.” Viewlocked aims to unify those experiences into a single planning environment while helping travellers discover local businesses that may otherwise be difficult to find. The bootstrapped Viewlocked, which recently began conversations around a seed funding round to support product development, market expansion, and growth initiatives, has built a global network of travel partners across multiple destinations before launch, entirely through founder-led outreach and without paid marketing. “As a recently launched platform, our focus has been on building both supply and community,” Ola said. “We’ve onboarded travel partners across accommodations, tours, transportation, diving, surfing, wellness, and local experiences, while also attracting travellers, travel creators, and early adopters who are actively exploring the platform.” The response from both partners and travelers has been “encouraging”, and continues to validate demand for a more connected travel planning experience. “Viewlocked currently features partners across Morocco, Indonesia, Spain, Egypt, Sri Lanka, the Philippines, Norway, Colombia, Croatia, Tanzania, Thailand, Zimbabwe, France, the Maldives, and Mexico,” said Ola. “Our strategy is to continue expanding destination coverage globally by onboarding high-quality local travel businesses and experiences.” Long term, Ola envisions Viewlocked becoming a global platform that helps travellers discover and plan trips anywhere in the world. The startup operates on a commission-based model. “Partners can join and list their services for free, while Viewlocked earns a commission on successful bookings generated through the platform,” said Ola. “As a newly launched company, our primary focus is currently on growth, partner acquisition, traveler adoption, and platform development.”

title: Beltone Venture Capital Increases Investment in Egyptian Brands ariika and Lychee for Saudi Expansion
summary: Beltone Venture Capital (BVC) has increased its investment in Egyptian consumer brands ariika and Lychee to support their expansion into Saudi Arabia, with plans to open a combined five new stores in Riyadh.
content: Beltone Venture Capital (BVC), the venture capital arm of Beltone Holding, has increased its investment in Egyptian consumer brands ariika and Lychee to support their expansion into Saudi Arabia, where the two companies plan to open a combined five new stores in Riyadh. ariika is a direct-to-consumer home furnishing brand led by Khaled Attallah, which will launch two stores in Riyadh, while healthy food and beverage brand Lychee, founded by Mohamed Assy, will open three stores as part of its regional growth strategy. The move signals a decisive new chapter in BVC's strategy, not merely writing cheques but actively building homegrown Egyptian brands into becoming dominant regional players. "ariika and Lychee represent exactly what we look for – homegrown Egyptian brands with proven profitability, exceptional leadership, and the ambition to redefine their categories across the region. We are long-term partners in building the next generation of MENA consumer powerhouses. With a combined five new stores opening in Riyadh, this is just the beginning," said Ali Mokhtar, CEO of BVC. ariika founder and CEO Attallah said Saudi Arabia had always been central to his company's regional vision. "Opening two stores in Riyadh is not just an expansion, it is a statement. We have built a brand that resonates deeply with consumers who value design, quality, and an exceptional experience, and we are confident that ariika will find its home in the Saudi market just as it has across Egypt and Iraq. This is the next chapter, and we are only getting started," he said.

title: Kenya's Fikra Launches AI Inference API for African Developers, Startups, and Businesses
summary: Kenyan startup Fikra API has launched an OpenAI-compatible AI inference API tailored for African developers, featuring M-Pesa payments, local currency pricing, and proprietary models like Fikra Nano 1B. The platform addresses barriers of high upfront costs and lack of mobile money support from global providers.
content: Kenya's Fikra API has launched an AI inference API built specifically for African developers, startups, and businesses. Founded by James Miano earlier this year, Fikra API is an OpenAI-compatible inference API targeting African developers, with M-Pesa payments built in and pricing in local terms rather than USD. Developers can send requests to Fikra's endpoint and get responses from large language models, the same way they would with OpenAI or Anthropic, but with pricing, payment methods, and token economics designed for African markets. "If you are a developer or startup founder in Nairobi, Lagos, or Cape Town and you want to build an AI-powered product, the standard path is OpenAI or Anthropic. Both require a minimum spend of around US$20 before you can access the API," Miano told Disrupt Africa. "That figure sounds small, but in Nairobi US$20 is close to a week's salary for many people. For those who can afford it, the problem does not stop there, a frontier model output pricing is expensive and burns through tokens quickly, forcing constant top-ups that compound the cost." The second barrier is payment. "Most people in Africa are not cardholders. OpenAI and Anthropic require a credit or debit card. Mobile money is how the majority of people here actually transact, and none of the major AI API providers support it," Miano said. Fikra API exists to solve both. Fully-bootstrapped, the startup onboarded its first 10 users within days of launch. "We have also shipped two proprietary models – a custom embeddings model ready for inference, and Fikra Nano 1B which is a ternary weight fine-tuned model we built in-house. The ternary weight pipeline is a genuinely uncommon capability and gives us a technical foundation most API providers at this stage do not have," said Miano. Currently only active in Kenya, with payment infrastructure live via Paystack which covers much of sub-Saharan Africa, Fikra's model is built to scale continent-wide. "Any African developer with a smartphone and mobile money access is the target user," said Miano. The startup charges US$1 per two million tokens. "We do not split input and output pricing the way most providers do, it is one flat rate. Payment is via Paystack, which supports M-Pesa and mobile money across multiple African markets. We are pre-revenue at this stage with the commercial push beginning at launch," said Miano. "The main challenge is onboarding friction, getting a developer from signup to their first successful API call as fast as possible, because if that moment takes too long you lose them. We identified this early and have been iterating on it. The broader difficulty is distribution. The developers we are building for are not always in the same communities where tech press reaches, so finding and activating them requires ground-level work."

title: Village Capital distributes $500k grants to 32 climate startups across Africa
summary: Village Capital concludes its three-year Empowering Sustainable Entrepreneurship Africa (ESEA) initiative, deploying $500,000 in grant funding to 32 climate startups across Ghana, Kenya, Malawi, Mozambique, and Tanzania. The largest share went to climate adaptation startups ($225k), followed by food security ($125k), blue economy ($100k), and renewable energy ($50k).
content: Village Capital has announced the conclusion of the Empowering Sustainable Entrepreneurship Africa (ESEA), a three-year initiative established to strengthen locally-led entrepreneurial ecosystems and expand pathways to capital for climate startups across Africa. Village Capital has partnered Norad – the Norwegian Agency for Development Cooperation to run ESEA, which was designed around a core belief that entrepreneurs do not scale in isolation. Through ESEA, Village Capital worked with 14 locally-led ecosystem builders across Ghana, Kenya, Malawi, Mozambique, and Tanzania to strengthen their organisations and test a co-delivery model for founder support. Building on this foundation, it partnered with five ecosystem builders in 2025 to co-deliver investment-readiness programmes for 150 climate startups across the five markets. As the initiative concludes, Village Capital has deployed US$500,000 in grant funding to 32 startups addressing critical sustainability challenges across the continent. The largest share of funding was directed toward climate adaptation startups, which received US$225,000. Food security startups received US$125,000, blue economy startups received US$100,000, and renewable energy startups received US$50,000. “At Village Capital, we’ve learned that the challenge isn’t a lack of entrepreneurial talent. Too often, the challenge is that the systems around entrepreneurs are not designed to help promising startups access the networks, resources, and capital they need to grow,” said Nakami Walunywa, regional director for Africa at Village Capital. “ESEA gave us an opportunity to test what happens when you work through local organisations that are deeply embedded in their markets. We saw how local knowledge, trusted founder relationships, and ecosystem intelligence can help surface stronger pipelines and create more effective pathways between entrepreneurs and capital. Those lessons are shaping how we think about ecosystem building, not as a parallel effort to investing, but as essential infrastructure for directing capital where it can create the greatest long-term value.”

title: Nigerian startup StableKlin launches behavioural intelligence platform for cleaning businesses
summary: Nigerian startup StableKlin has developed a behavioural intelligence platform that helps cleaning businesses and recurring service providers detect early signs of customer disengagement by analyzing booking patterns and behavioural changes. Founded by Dehinde Sogbanmu, the self-funded platform aims to improve customer retention by identifying subtle shifts in customer behaviour before they impact revenue.
content: Nigerian startup StableKlin is a behavioural intelligence platform designed for cleaning businesses and other recurring service providers, which helps business owners understand something that is often difficult to see until it is too late – changes in customer behaviour. Most cleaning businesses already track bookings, customer details, invoices and payments. “What they often do not have is a clear way to identify when a loyal customer is gradually becoming less engaged. Customers rarely announce that they are leaving. More often, they start booking less frequently, delay appointments, become less predictable, or quietly drift away over time,” said Dehinde Sogbanmu, StableKlin’s founder. StableKlin is built to detect those early warning signs. The platform analyses patterns such as a customer’s usual booking frequency, the time since their last service, changes in scheduling habits, consistency over time, and other behavioural indicators. It then highlights customers whose behaviour is beginning to move away from what has historically been normal for them. “For example, if a customer typically books every four weeks but has not booked for three months, that may indicate more than a simple delay. It could be an early sign that the relationship is weakening,” said Sogbanmu. “The goal is to give business owners greater visibility into customer stability so they can take action earlier, strengthen retention, and reduce the risk of losing valuable repeat business.” StableKlin was born from a real-world problem Sogbanmu encountered while running Potteries Prime Cleaning, a cleaning business in the UK. “As I worked with recurring customers, I noticed a pattern – customers rarely disappeared overnight. In most cases, there were subtle behavioural changes beforehand. Booking intervals became longer, routines became less consistent, and engagement gradually declined before revenue was affected,” he said. “I realised that while there were plenty of tools available to help manage operations, there were very few tools focused on understanding customer behaviour itself.” That insight led him to begin developing StableKlin. Rather than focusing on operational management, the startup focuses on behavioural insight. “It helps business owners identify changes in customer patterns before those changes become visible in revenue figures,” said Sogbanmu. Currently self-funded, StableKlin has deliberately taken a lean approach to development, focusing on validating the core problem before investing heavily in additional features or infrastructure. “One of the most significant milestones so far has been transforming the concept from an observation made while running a cleaning business into a working product with a functional dashboard,” said Sogbanmu. “The platform can already analyse customer booking behaviour, identify deviations from normal patterns and surface behavioural insights that may indicate customer drift. Another important milestone has been beginning conversations with business owners and sharing the broader vision publicly. This includes publishing content around behavioural intelligence in service businesses, engaging with founders and operators, and introducing the concept to industry and technology audiences.” At this stage, the focus remains on learning, refining the product and ensuring that the insights generated are genuinely useful to the businesses it is designed to serve. “The platform has a working dashboard and behavioural analysis capabilities, and I am actively seeking cleaning-business owners and service operators who are willing to test the concept and provide feedback,” said Sogbanmu. “Although it is still early, the response to the idea itself has been encouraging. Many business owners immediately recognise the problem because they have experienced it firsthand. They understand that customers often do not leave suddenly; instead, their behaviour changes gradually before they eventually stop booking.” StableKlin is currently focused on the UK cleaning industry. “I chose this market because it is where I have direct operational experience and a deep understanding of the challenges faced by business owners. Cleaning businesses rely heavily on trust, consistency and recurring customer relationships, making them an ideal starting point for behavioural analysis,” said Sogbanmu. “Looking ahead, I believe the same principles can be applied across a much wider range of recurring service industries. Potential future markets include facilities management, maintenance services, home services, care-related businesses and other sectors where customer retention plays a critical role in long-term growth.”

title: South African startup Supascale launches Africa's first GPU cloud marketplace
summary: Supascale has launched Africa's first GPU cloud marketplace, making advanced AI models more affordable while enabling GPU owners to monetize unused computing power through a green alternative that utilizes idle GPUs from laptops, PCs, and data centers.
content: South African startup Supascale has launched Africa's first GPU cloud marketplace, making advanced AI models more affordable while allowing GPU owners to earn passive income. Founded by Aaron Bornmann, Supascale enables users to access leading AI models at significantly lower costs, while having the world's fastest GPU onboarding process for GPU owners to monetise unused computing power through a cloud AI marketplace. As demand for AI continues to surge globally, and AI data centres are putting increasing strain on the environment through excessive noise, electricity and water resources usage for cooling, Supascale offers a green-alternative to accessing AI models powered by idle GPUs from laptops, PCs and unused GPUs in data centres – allowing everyday people to earn from the AI boom. "The AI economy needs to be accessible to everyone, not just large hyperscalers," said Bornmann. "We're building the infrastructure that allows businesses, developers, creators, and innovators to access world-class AI capabilities while enabling everyday GPU owners to participate directly in the AI economy." The company plans to expand its footprint internationally while positioning Africa as an active participant in the rapidly growing global AI infrastructure market.

title: Pan-African WRAPPED Helping People Find Jobs in Film Industry
summary: WRAPPED, a platform founded by Zoe Ramushu, centralizes job hiring in the film and television industry, replacing fragmented WhatsApp and Facebook groups. The startup gained 600 sign-ups in its first days and secured a partnership with MultiChoice, aiming to be a global solution built by Africans.
content: “WRAPPED is the app that I wish I had when I was trying to break into this industry. Right now, hiring in film and television is done through WhatsApp groups, Facebook groups, spreadsheets, and institutional memory captured in human brains,” WRAPPED founder Zoe Ramushu tells Disrupt Africa. “A message drops in a WhatsApp group, a gig is gone in two minutes. You didn’t even know what that job was. Two minutes.” Ramushu has come up with a solution. What WRAPPED does is give the industry one centralised place where everybody is, where all the jobs are getting booked out of, at the click of a button. “We’re categorised by skills and experience level so you find exactly who you’re looking for. We cut down 33 per cent of the pre-production workload. It sounds simple and it is, that’s the innovation,” Ramushu said. She said the idea for WRAPPED came up based on her own experiences. “I co-founded Swift (Sisters Working in Film and Television) and spent five-to-six years voluntarily building that organisation because I believe in being part of the change I’d like to see. Then I got my first Netflix project. Close to 120 people on set. I said to my line producer, we need at least one female in every department. Silence in the room. Like I was joking. We found them, a female in every single department. But it was an exercise,” said Ramushu. “Time-consuming. In production, time is literally money. That was the moment I said this cannot be the way things work. WRAPPED was real and existed in my mind from that moment. And glad today it’s now real for everyone else.” The startup had 600 sign-ups in the first two to three days, with minimal marketing. “That just spoke to the desire, the complete demand, for people working in this industry to have a centralised place to gather. A place that allows them to connect and be seen,” said Ramushu. “Our partnership with MultiChoice has been invaluable. They’ve been championing African stories for decades and having meaningful funding as well as their support has really just changed the shape of what we’re building.” Ramushu said she doesn’t see WRAPPED as an African platform. “It is built by Africans but it is a global platform. If it’s good tech, it’s good tech. If it’s a good product, it’s a good product. Same as film. Our presence at Berlin, at Cannes, three years running, those invitations are evidence that we are global. That’s not a plan. That’s already happening,” she said. The platform is free for freelancers and agents, production companies can choose a subscription that best suits their needs.

title: African Tech Startups Raise $260M in Q2 2026, Down 40% Year-on-Year
summary: According to Disrupt Africa's 11th annual African Tech Startups Funding Report, 38 African tech startups raised $260 million in Q2 2026, a 40% decline from $427 million in Q2 2025. This raises concerns of a funding dip after 2025's 50% recovery to $1.64 billion. Q1 2026 saw $382.15 million across 40 startups. Extrapolated full-year 2026 projections: 156 startups and $1.28 billion, down from 178 startups and $1.6 billion in 2025.
content: Thirty-eight African tech startups raised a total of US$260 million in the second quarter of 2026, down 40 per cent on the US$427 million raised in the same period of 2025. Total investment into the African tech startup ecosystem increased by almost 50 per cent to US$1.64 billion in 2025 as the sector began to slowly recover from the impacts of the global capital shortage, according to the 11th edition of the annual African Tech Startups Funding Report released by startup news and research portal Disrupt Africa. Numbers from Q1 2026 had suggested this year would at least match, and probably surpass, 2025, with 40 startups banking US$382,150,000 in funding. Yet in Q2, 38 startups raised just $260 million, substantially down from the same period last year, and raising concerns African tech could be in for another funding dip. Extrapolated over the course of the year, that would be 156 startups (down from 178 in 2025), and an annual 2026 total of US$1.28 billion, down from US$1.6 billion in 2025.

title: Female-led startups invited to apply for AWIEF's annual Pitch n Grow competition
summary: The Africa Women Innovation and Entrepreneurship Forum (AWIEF) is inviting female-led startups across Africa to apply for Pitch n Grow, its flagship annual pitch competition program. The 2026 edition focuses on Deep Technology and Africa's unique advantages in local knowledge and community understanding. Applications are open until July 20, 2026.
content: Female-led startups have been invited to apply for Pitch n Grow, AWIEF's flagship annual women entrepreneurship pitch competition programme, designed to identify, develop, and amplify the most promising women-led ventures across Africa. The Africa Women Innovation and Entrepreneurship Forum (AWIEF) runs Pitch n Grow each year, aiming to empower founders to accelerate their growth and expand their impact through mentorship, training, visibility, and access to opportunities. Every edition of Pitch n Grow adopts a strategic theme aligned with AWIEF's continental priorities and vision for Africa's future. "For 2026, we are shining a spotlight on Deep Technology and the unique advantage Africa already possesses – deep knowledge of local contexts, deep understanding of communities, and deep experience of challenges the rest of the world is yet to face," organisers said. Applications are open until July 20.

title: Stablecoins Are Not Killing the Naira, They Are Keeping Nigerian Businesses Alive
summary: Mbah Casmir, founder and CEO of Monica Cash, argues that stablecoins like USDT and USDC are not threats to the naira but essential tools for Nigerian businesses, freelancers, and remote workers to manage currency volatility, preserve value, and facilitate international payments. With $92.1 billion in crypto transactions in 2024, Nigeria's stablecoin adoption is driven by utility, not speculation.
content: The conversation around stablecoins in Nigeria often starts from the wrong premise, writes Mbah Casmir, founder and CEO of Monica Cash, a cryptocurrency-to-naira exchange platform helping individuals and businesses across Nigeria convert digital assets seamlessly. For years, digital assets, particularly dollar-backed stablecoins such as USDT and USDC, have been framed as threats to the naira. Critics argue that their growing adoption encourages capital flight, weakens confidence in the local currency, and accelerates dollarisation. While those concerns may sound reasonable, they overlook what is actually happening across Nigeria’s economy. The reality is that most Nigerians are not turning to stablecoins because they want to speculate on cryptocurrency. They are turning to them because they are looking for practical ways to navigate economic uncertainty, preserve value, and keep businesses running. For thousands of small businesses, importers, exporters, freelancers, and remote workers, stablecoins have quietly become part of everyday financial planning. Consider the experience of a small importer sourcing products from suppliers abroad. A transaction that looks profitable on Monday can easily become a loss before payment is finally settled. For many businesses operating on tight margins, that kind of volatility makes it extremely difficult to plan with confidence. Every delay creates uncertainty, and every exchange rate movement can directly affect profitability. Stablecoins have become one of the tools helping businesses manage that risk. Rather than holding funds in a currency experiencing rapid fluctuations, many business owners temporarily preserve value in digital dollars until transactions are completed. This is not an attempt to replace the naira. It is a response to the realities of operating in a highly volatile financial environment. The same pattern is playing out among Nigerian freelancers and remote professionals. Nigeria has become one of Africa’s leading hubs for digital talent, with professionals increasingly working for clients across North America, Europe, and other international markets. Yet receiving and managing international payments through traditional channels can still involve delays, friction, and conversion challenges. Stablecoins solves this problem by providing a faster and more flexible alternative that allows many professionals to exercise greater control over their earnings. This trend is not unique to Nigeria. Across emerging markets experiencing currency pressures, stablecoins are increasingly functioning as financial infrastructure. They are helping individuals and businesses preserve purchasing power, and participate more effectively in the global economy. What makes Nigeria particularly remarkable is the scale of adoption. According to recent blockchain analytics data, Nigerians processed approximately $92.1 billion in cryptocurrency transactions in 2024, making the country one of the most active crypto markets globally. A significant portion of that activity was driven by stablecoins, which tells an important story about how digital assets are being used in practice. Much of the demand is being driven by utility rather than speculation. The narrative that stablecoins are undermining the naira also overlooks a more important question: why are businesses turning to them in the first place? People generally adopt solutions that solve real problems. If stablecoin adoption continues to grow, it is because businesses and individuals are finding genuine value in the speed, accessibility, and financial flexibility these tools provide. In many cases, stablecoins are helping businesses protect working capital, manage risk more effectively, and maintain operational stability despite ongoing currency volatility. That growing reliance on stablecoins is also why companies like Monica.Cash have emerged, because the challenge for many users is no longer accessing stablecoins, but converting them quickly, securely, and efficiently whenever business needs arise. Monica.Cash was built to help bridge that gap by making stablecoin-to-naira transactions faster, simpler, and more accessible for individuals and businesses that increasingly rely on digital assets as part of their financial activities. Conversations within Monica.Cash, including ongoing discussions with COO, Chinazam Umezinwa, consistently reveal that much of the demand being seen today is no longer being driven by speculation. More often than not, users are looking for practical financial solutions. They are entrepreneurs trying to protect margins, professionals receiving payments from abroad, and businesses seeking more predictable ways to manage transactions in a challenging economic climate. This is one of the reasons the stablecoin conversation deserves greater nuance. Stablecoins and national currencies should not automatically be viewed as competitors. In many instances, they are serving different purposes within the sam

title: Catalyst Fund Announces $30M Second Close to Deepen Investment in Africa's Climate Resilience Founders
summary: Pan-African venture fund Catalyst Fund has secured a $30 million second close, bringing total commitments closer to its target. The fund backs pre-seed to Series A startups building climate adaptation solutions for underserved African communities. New investors include IFC, FASA, Shell Foundation, Trafigura Foundation, Speedinvest, BlinkCV, and high-net-worth individuals, joining earlier backers FSD Africa and Cisco Foundation.
content: Catalyst Fund, the pan-African venture fund and venture builder backing founders from pre-seed to Series A, has announced a US$30 million second close, bringing it closer to its target and adding fresh momentum to its mission of strengthening climate resilience across the continent. Catalyst Fund is a pre-seed VC fund and accelerator backing high-impact tech startups that seek to improve the resilience of underserved, climate-vulnerable communities. It partners with mission-driven founders that share our vision of a world where every individual has the tools and opportunities they need to thrive. Until 2023, the organisation offered grant capital to selected startups, but it has since switched to an equity-based model. Its first close of that fund was in September 2023, and the second close brings together IFC, FASA, Shell Foundation, Trafigura Foundation, Speedinvest, BlinkCV, and a group of high-net-worth individuals, joining early backers FSD Africa, Cisco Foundation, and others who validated the thesis at first close. Catalyst Fund expects to reach final close later this year. “Climate adaptation is one of the defining investment themes of the next decade, especially in Africa, where the need is immediate, and the entrepreneurial talent is extraordinary. This second close allows us to double down on our mission: backing ambitious founders building practical, scalable solutions for a climate-changed world, and supporting them not just with capital, but with the hands-on venture-building support they need to grow,” said Maelis Carraro, founder and general partner at Catalyst Fund.

title: La condamnation de l'homme d'affaires Marouane Mabrouk confirmée en appel
summary: La Chambre criminelle spécialisée dans les affaires de corruption financière près la Cour d'appel de Tunis a confirmé le jugement de première instance condamnant l'homme d'affaires Marouane Mabrouk à 14 ans de prison pour blanchiment d'argent, détournement de fonds publics et complicité d'abus de pouvoir.
content: La Chambre criminelle spécialisée dans les affaires de corruption financière près la Cour d'appel de Tunis a confirmé le jugement de première instance condamnant l'homme d'affaires Marouane Mabrouk à 14 ans de prison pour blanchiment d'argent.
